title: When the Storm Hits
subtitle: Tail risk, black swans & contagion

hook.metaphor: Most days the sea is manageable. But every so often comes the freak wave — bigger than the forecast said was possible, arriving when several bad things line up at once. Ships that only prepared for *average* weather sink in these. Market risk's deadliest moments aren't the daily chop; they're the rare, violent storms that the normal models swore couldn't happen.
hook.scenario: In 2008, assets that 'never' fell together all crashed at once; in early 2020, markets dropped faster than almost any model predicted. Firms that relied on 'normal' assumptions were stunned. What do these events reveal about the limits of everyday risk measures?

learn: **Tail risk** is the danger living in the extreme 'tail' of outcomes — the rare, severe losses beyond what normal measures like VaR describe (the 1% we flagged in Level 3). These events are *unlikely* but *devastating* — and they're where firms actually die.

A **'black swan'** is an event so rare and unexpected it wasn't in anyone's model — yet obvious in hindsight. The lesson isn't to predict them (you can't) but to stay resilient enough to survive them: don't bet the whole firm on 'this has never happened'.

Crises bring **correlation breakdown**: in calm times, diversification works because assets move independently; in a panic, *everything crashes together* ('all correlations go to 1'). The diversification that protected you on Tuesday can vanish exactly when you need it most (2008's lesson).

Storms also expose the deadly link to **liquidity risk** (its own track): when you're forced to sell into a falling market and there are no buyers, paper losses become real, fire-sale losses. Market and liquidity risk feed each other in a downward spiral.

Which is why **stress testing and scenario analysis** matter more than any single number: deliberately imagining the freak wave ('what if our worst historical crisis repeats, only worse?') and making sure you'd survive it. You prepare for the storm in the calm — because in the storm it's too late to start.
bridge: So at work, you can puncture dangerous over-confidence with one question: *what happens to us in the freak storm, not the average day?* Treat 'it's never happened' as a warning, not a comfort; remember diversification can fail in a panic; and value the stress test that imagines the unthinkable. Surviving the rare gale, not optimising for calm seas, is what keeps the firm afloat.

interactions.title: 'It's never happened before'
interactions.prompt: A team dismisses a scenario because 'markets have never moved that far'. How should you respond?

options.label: Agree — if it's never happened, it won't.
options.feedback: This is the exact reasoning that sinks firms in black-swan events. 'Never happened' is not 'cannot happen' — 2008 and 2020 both broke prior records. Past calm guarantees nothing.

options.label: Treat 'never happened' as a reason to stress-test it, not dismiss it.
options.feedback: Exactly. The dangerous losses live in the tail beyond past experience. You deliberately model the freak wave and ensure you'd survive it — resilience over prediction.

options.label: Panic and sell everything immediately.
options.feedback: Over-reaction isn't the answer either. You don't liquidate in fear; you prepare — stress test, set limits, keep buffers — so a storm is survivable, not fatal.

interactions.title: Storm vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each concept to its meaning.

pairs.right: Rare, severe losses beyond normal measures
An extreme event no model foresaw
In a panic, assets crash together (diversification fails)
Forced selling into a falling market with no buyers
Deliberately modelling a severe scenario to test survival

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “The deadliest market risk isn't the daily chop but the rare freak wave — tail events where everything crashes together and 'it's never happened' stops being comfort — so you stress-test the unthinkable and keep buffers to survive it.”

options: Rare but severe losses beyond what normal measures capture
checkpoint.explain: It's the extreme end of outcomes — unlikely but devastating, and where firms actually fail.

checkpoint.q: In a severe crisis, diversification can fail because…
options: Assets that normally move independently can all crash together
checkpoint.explain: Correlations 'go to 1' in a panic — the protection vanishes exactly when you need it most.

checkpoint.q: The right way to handle 'it's never happened before' is to…
options: Treat it as proof it can't happen
Stress-test the scenario and ensure you'd survive it
checkpoint.explain: Past records get broken; resilience to the freak wave beats false comfort from history.
